ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn class exists for cheese and pork jerky toast specifically; mapped to the closest parent class 'toast (food product)' as an approximate anchor.
ontology.foodex2_description: Bread and similar products — composite bread-based dish with meat and cheese toppings
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not have a specific code for Singaporean-style cheese and pork jerky toast; A000E (bread and similar products) used as the closest defensible parent node.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for this composite bread-based dish; left null to preserve ontology honesty.

serving.natural_language_portion: One typical serving is approximately one slice of cheese and pork jerky toast, weighing around 80 g, as commonly served at Singaporean bakeries and cafes.

nutrient_source.title: Cheese & Pork Jerky Toast — HPB Singapore Foodo Nutrient Data
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values taken directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for Cheese & Pork Jerky Toast (cr_id G00010098). All per_100g values preserved from the canonical HPB record. Saturated fat estimated at approximately 11.5 g/100g based on ingredient composition (cheddar cheese ~6 g/100g contribution + pork jerky ~5.5 g/100g contribution); this field is not explicitly reported in the HPB record and is documented as an estimate.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — white bread and composite bread-based products
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Singaporean cheese and pork jerky toast. GI estimated at 70 based on the closest analogous food: white bread / milk toast (GI ~70–75, University of Sydney GI Database). The low carbohydrate content (8.85 g/100g) results in a low glycemic load per 80 g serving: GL = (70 × 8.85 × 0.80) / 100 = 4.97. The high protein (16.09 g) and fat (23.06 g) content will further attenuate the postprandial glucose response in practice.

hypertension.key_factor: Extremely high sodium at 1279 mg/100g — more than double the 600 mg/100g threshold — driven by processed cheddar cheese, pork jerky (bak kwa), and salted bread dough
hypertension.guidance: Patients with hypertension should avoid this food; a single 80 g serving delivers approximately 1023 mg sodium, representing 68% of the WHO recommended daily limit of 2000 mg, and regular consumption poses a serious risk of blood pressure elevation and cardiovascular events.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low net carbohydrate load (8.85 g/100g) partially offset by zero dietary fibre, high saturated fat (~11.5 g/100g) which may worsen insulin resistance, and added sugars in pork jerky
type2_diabetes.guidance: While the glycemic load per serving is low (GL ~5), patients with type 2 diabetes should exercise caution due to the high saturated fat content that may impair insulin sensitivity over time, the high sodium burden, and the added sugars present in pork jerky; strict portion control and infrequent consumption are advised.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: High total fat (23.06 g/100g), estimated saturated fat (~11.5 g/100g), trans fat (0.1 g/100g), cholesterol (78 mg/100g), and extreme sodium (1279 mg/100g) collectively represent a significant cardiovascular risk profile
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Patients with cardiovascular disease or dyslipidaemia should avoid this food; the combination of high saturated fat, trans fat, dietary cholesterol, and extreme sodium substantially elevates LDL cholesterol, promotes arterial inflammation, and raises blood pressure, all of which are independent risk factors for major adverse cardiovascular events.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: High saturated fat (~11.5 g/100g) and processed red meat (pork jerky) rich in arachidonic acid amplify TNF-alpha and IL-6 pro-inflammatory pathways; trans fat (0.1 g/100g) further worsens systemic inflammation
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Patients with rheumatoid arthritis should avoid this food, particularly during flares; the combination of processed red meat, high saturated fat, trans fat, and extreme sodium promotes joint inflammation and tissue swelling through multiple pro-inflammatory mechanisms.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Cheese and Pork Jerky Toast is a Singaporean-Chinese bakery dish consisting of a thick slice of soft milk toast bread topped with savory pork jerky (bak kwa), crispy seaweed floss, homemade salad dressing, and a slice of cheddar cheese. The bread base is made from a milk toast dough enriched with fresh cream, giving it a soft, slightly chewy, and pillowy texture. The flavor profile balances sweet and salty notes — the sweetness of the pork jerky and milk bread contrasts with the savory umami of the cheddar cheese and seaweed floss. Per 100 g, the dish provides 307 kcal, 16.09 g protein, 23.06 g fat, and only 8.85 g carbohydrate, making it calorie-dense and high in fat relative to its carbohydrate content. Sodium is extremely high at 1279 mg/100g, driven by the combination of processed cheese, cured pork jerky, and salted bread. Calcium is notably high at 1375 mg/100g, primarily from the cheddar cheese. Clinically, this food is unsuitable for patients with hypertension due to its extreme sodium content, and poses significant cardiovascular risk due to high saturated fat, trans fat, and dietary cholesterol. It is also pro-inflammatory and should be avoided by patients with rheumatoid arthritis. For type 2 diabetes, the glycemic load per serving is low, but the high saturated fat content may worsen insulin resistance with regular consumption. This dish is commonly consumed as a lunch or dinner item in Singapore and is associated with Singaporean Chinese bakery culture.
common_user_queries: Is cheese and pork jerky toast healthy?
How much sodium is in bak kwa cheese toast?
Can diabetics eat cheese and pork jerky toast?

What are the calories in cheese bak kwa toast?
Is cheese pork jerky toast high in fat?
What is the glycemic index of milk toast with cheese and bak kwa?

confidence_reason: Nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo canonical database entry for this exact dish (cr_id G00010098, confidence 0.95). All core macros and secondary nutrients are present from the primary regional source. Saturated fat is estimated from ingredient composition and documented accordingly.
notes: 1. dietary_fibre_g is 0.0: confirmed zero per HPB Singapore Foodo database entry; the milk toast bread and processed toppings contribute negligible dietary fibre. 2. saturated_fat_g (11.5 g/100g) is an ingredient-composition estimate: the HPB record does not explicitly report saturated fat; the value is derived from the known saturated fat content of cheddar cheese (~6 g/100g contribution) and pork jerky (~5.5 g/100g contribution) and is flagged as estimated. 3. trans_fat_g (0.1 g/100g) is noted in the input health_suitability section as present; this is consistent with processed cheese and pork jerky ingredients and is retained as an approximate value. 4. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, water_g, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg set to -1: detailed fatty acid fractions, moisture, and micronutrient data beyond those reported are not available in the HPB Singapore Foodo record for this dish as of 2026. 5. GI of 70 is an analogous estimate based on white bread / milk toast from the University of Sydney GI Database; no direct GI measurement for this specific composite dish exists in any approved source. 6. Calcium value of 1375 mg/100g is exceptionally high and is preserved as reported by HPB; this is plausible given the substantial cheddar cheese content and should be verified against the original HPB laboratory analysis if used in clinical contexts. 7. This dish is not halal due to pork jerky content.
